bibliographic.citation: An Alphabet of Tales: An English 15th Century Translation of the Alphabetum Narrationum of Etienne de Besancon, from Additional MS. 25,719 of the British Museum, ed. Mary Macleod Banks, 2 parts, EETS Original Series 126-127 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner & Co., 1904-1905).
bibliographic.pages: [v] + 532 pages in two parts, plus errata
bibliographic.publisher: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner & Co., Ltd.
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 126-127
bibliographic.title: An Alphabet of Tales

entities.description: The immediate print source: a complete two-part transcription of Add MS 25719. Banks restores worn or obliterated text conjecturally in brackets, distinguishes inadvertently omitted supplies with bracketed italics, modernizes punctuation, selectively regularizes capitalization, corrects scribal errors in footnotes, and compares difficult readings with Harley MS 268 and Arundel MS 378.
entities.label: Banks's An Alphabet of Tales
notes: Part I covers A-H and printed pages 1-260; Part II covers I-Z and pages 261-532.
Part II includes a reduced photographic facsimile of Add MS 25719, f. 131r, as its frontispiece; this is a single selected manuscript leaf, not a complete manuscript reproduction.
The promised Part III introduction, glossary, index, and general clearing-up was not included in these two parts and was not found as a later publication.
Part II changes the subtitle to 'once attributed to Etienne de Besancon' after Herbert's 1905 authorship article.

rights.basis: The two parts were first published in 1904 and 1905, more than the 95-year maximum term described by current U.S. Copyright Office guidance for pre-1978 statutory copyrights; the Internet Archive item metadata also labels both scans NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT.
rights.component: Banks's 1904-1905 transcription, notes, editorial interventions, and errata

holding.notes: The translation occupies ff. 1r-184v; f. 185 is a flyleaf from a Latin service book.

entities.description: A Latin comparison witness used by Banks for difficult and confused readings. Herbert describes the complete 117-folio parchment codex as an early-fifteenth-century Alphabetum narrationum formerly belonging to the Carthusians near Mainz.

entities.description: The early-fourteenth-century Latin exemplum compilation translated into Middle English. This conceptual source-work entity does not stand for one identified surviving Latin manuscript or claim that the English translator used Harley MS 268 or Arundel MS 378.

notes: The source edition's Etienne de Besancon attribution is historical and rejected; current specialist sources identify Arnold of Liege.

reviewNotes: The cataloging subject is the Middle English translation, not Arnold of Liege's Latin compilation, Banks's edition, or the manuscript as a physical object.
Etienne de Besancon is retained only as a rejected historical attribution and as part of the 1904 bibliographic title.
Harley MS 268 and Arundel MS 378 are Banks's explicitly named Latin comparison copies; they are not asserted to be the anonymous medieval translator's exemplar.
Add MS 25719 contains the English work at ff. 1r-184v and a distinct service-book flyleaf at f. 185. A future facsimile request should cover the entire physical codex, not only the work leaves.
Banks's 1905 Part II frontispiece reproduces only Add MS 25719, f. 131r. It is useful paleographical evidence but must not be presented as a complete manuscript facsimile.
A blank Digitised Content field and negative public searches are date-bounded evidence, not proof that no private, internal, or unindexed surrogate exists.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is the pinned CME copy of a 1997 University of Michigan electronic text derived from Mary Macleod Banks's complete two-part EETS edition (1904-1905). Banks directly transcribed the anonymous fifteenth-century Northern Middle English translation in British Library Add MS 25719 and compared difficult readings with the Latin Alphabetum narrationum in Harley MS 268 and Arundel MS 378. The Latin source is now attributed to Arnold of Liege rather than Etienne de Besancon. Complete scans of both print volumes and the principal supporting scholarship are cached locally. Part II preserves one reduced facsimile of Add MS 25719, f. 131r, but no public complete manuscript facsimile was verified; the manifest therefore records a whole-codex British Library reproduction route and maps the work to ff. 1r-184v within the manuscript.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous translator (from Arnold of Liege)
catalogSummary.region: Northern England; the Ribble Valley and Salley (Sawley) Abbey are a later scholarly proposal
catalogSummary.title: An Alphabet of Tales

catalogingSubject.label: An Alphabet of Tales
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous fifteenth-century Middle English translation of Arnold of Liege's Latin Alphabetum narrationum, preserved complete in British Library Add MS 25719. This subject excludes Arnold's Latin compilation, the physical manuscript, Mary Macleod Banks's 1904-1905 two-volume edition, the U-M electronic encoding, and the repository XML artifact.

contentParts.label: Alphabetically arranged tales
notes: Banks's two editorial notes, the Explicit division, and the errata are edition or witness matter rather than separate intellectual tale parts.
contentParts.summary: The complete English translation runs from the subject Abbas through Ypocrisis and is organized by Latin alphabetical headings, individual tale rubrics, and cross-references.

assertion.notes: The surviving manuscript is fifteenth-century and Herbert dates it to about the middle of the century. EETS conventionally advertised the translation as about 1440, while later linguistic literature often uses c. 1450; no exact translation date was established.
Banks published the complete translation in two EETS parts, A-H in 1904 and I-Z in 1905.

extent.description: Encoding metric: 802 numbered tale DIV1 units, comprising Roman numerals I-DCCCI plus an inserted LXVIIIa. The XML has 806 DIV1 elements in all: two editorial notes, 803 chapter-typed divisions (the 802 tales and a separate Explicit division), and one errata division. The four-line Latin scribal colophon in the Explicit division is excluded from work-form and verse totals.

formStatements.description: An alphabetically organized prose collection; brief quotations and the four-line Latin scribal colophon do not change the whole-work form.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The translation is in a Northern Middle English variety.

languageStatements.label: Latin
notes: The XML marks 1,062 FOREIGN elements as Latin; that is an encoding metric, not a count of distinct intellectual parts.

scope.description: Latin thematic rubrics, source labels, quotations, cross-references, and the scribal colophon

languageStatements.label: French
notes: The XML marks one FOREIGN element as French; French is not a co-primary language of the translation.

scope.description: One short French quotation encoded within a tale

notes: The historical source-edition spelling 'Etienne de Besancon' is retained without diacritics in supplied English metadata for interoperability; the edition scans use Etienne de Besancon and the XML uses Etienne de Besancon in normalized bibliographic data.
The repository XML's language header lists only Latin and French because Middle English is the unmarked base language of the text.

next_steps: Consult Johnson's full dissertation through lawful access and compare its linguistic dataset with current LALME or LAEME localization.
Inspect Add MS 25719 or a complete facsimile for codicological evidence distinguishing translation, exemplar, and copying dates.
openQuestions.question: Can the date and place of the Middle English translation be established more securely than the conventional c. 1440-c. 1450 Northern dating and the Salley Abbey/Ribble-area proposal reported by Miura from Johnson?

next_steps: Consult the 2015 Brilli edition and specialist transmission studies for an English-version stemmatic analysis.
Do not treat Banks's comparison copies Harley MS 268 and Arundel MS 378 as the translator's exemplars without direct evidence.
openQuestions.question: Who translated An Alphabet of Tales, and which Latin manuscript or textual branch supplied the translation?

assertion.notes: Miura reports the mid-fifteenth-century Salley (Sawley) Abbey/Ribble-area proposal from Johnson. The proposal is useful but remains indirect here because Johnson's full restricted dissertation was not inspected.

notes: Neither the translator nor the exact Latin exemplar used for the translation has been identified in the sources reviewed.

scope.description: Middle English translation of Arnold of Liege's Latin Alphabetum narrationum
Banks's 1904-1905 scholarly transcription and apparatus, the immediate source edition for the U-M encoding

notes: The two Latin manuscripts are Banks's comparison witnesses; this does not establish either as the anonymous medieval translator's exemplar.

scope.description: Comparison of difficult or confused English readings against Latin copies in Harley MS 268 and Arundel MS 378

summaries.value: An anonymous Northern Middle English translation of Arnold of Liege's Alphabetum narrationum, an alphabetically indexed repertory of short moral and religious narratives intended especially for preaching and instruction. The English witness lacks the Latin prologue but preserves Latin subject rubrics, cross-references, and source labels around more than eight hundred prose tale divisions.

The source edition's attribution to Etienne de Besancon is historical. Banks's 1905 second title page already says 'once attributed to', and current specialist resources identify Arnold of Liege as compiler of the Latin source. Arnold is not the identified author or translator of the anonymous Middle English expression.

notes: This is the uniform title in the U-M header, not a medieval manuscript title.
The 1904 Part I title page still names Etienne de Besancon without the qualification 'once attributed to'.

scope.description: Banks's revised title wording on the 1905 Part II title page

titles.value: An Alphabet of Tales: An English 15th Century Translation of the Alphabetum Narrationum Once Attributed to Etienne de Besancon, from Additional MS. 25,719 of the British Museum

notes: The opening Latin rubric is followed by the Middle English tale; the Latin source prologue is absent from the English witness.
titles.value: Abbas non debet esse nimis rigidus. Vnde Anselmus. Som tyme ther was ane abbott...

work.author: Anonymous translator (from Arnold of Liege)

publication.embeddedTitle: Alphabetum narrationum. English — AN ALPHABET OF TALES.

work.regionDisplay: Northern England; the Ribble Valley and Salley (Sawley) Abbey are a later scholarly proposal
